segments.s409-g41: چو introduces a conditional 'if'. او را is the object pronoun 'him' with را marking it as definite/direct object. به بستن is an infinitival construction 'to bind/confine', and نباشد (negative of باشد) functions impersonally here: 'it is not' or 'it would not be'. روا is a predicative adjective 'proper/permissible'. Put together: 'If it would not be proper to bind him...'.
segments.s409-g42: چنین qualifies the following noun phrase ('such/this kind of'); بد 'bad' is opposed to خوب 'good' (here the contrast is explicit: 'bad, not good'). آید is the verb 'comes/happens' and از پادشا is the prepositional phrase 'from the king' (i.e. 'coming from the king'). The whole clause says that such a thing would be bad/ill-suited coming from the king.
segments.s409-g43: ولیکن is a discourse connective 'but/yet'. نباید is impersonal modal 'one must not / it must not be'. شکستن دلم is a noun phrase 'the breaking of my heart' (شکستن as a verbal noun + دلم 'my heart'). The final م is an archaic/existential/copulative element here (linked to the impersonal construction): overall 'but it must not be that my heart is broken.'
segments.s409-g44: که introduces a clause of consequence/reason ('for/that'); چون here means 'if/when' (conditional). بشکنی is second-person singular subjunctive/past-formal of 'to break' => 'if you break'. دل 'heart' is the object. ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from', جان 'life/soul'. بگسلم is first-person present/future-subjunctive from گسَل(یدن) 'to part/come apart' meaning 'I will break away / separate myself'. The clause expresses the consequence: 'for if you break my heart, I shall part from life.'
segments.s409-g45: چگونه = 'how'. کشم is first-person present/subjunctive of کشیدن 'to draw/pull' used rhetorically here ('how could I...'). سر is the direct object 'head' (placed before the verb for emphasis). ز فرمان شاه = 'from the king's command' (ز = from; فرمان شاه = the king's order). The phrase asks rhetorically how the speaker could oppose or lift his head against the king's command.
segments.s409-g46: چگونه again 'how'. گذارم is first-person present/subjunctive of گذارد/گذاشتن in the sense 'to leave/abandon' (here 'how could I relinquish'). چنین دستگاه = 'such a system/establishment/court' (چنین 'such'; دستگاه 'establishment, court, apparatus'). The line asks how the speaker could abandon such an order or set-up.
segments.s409-g47: مرا‌گر is an older conditional compound meaning 'if to me' or 'if it were to me'; it introduces the possibility. به زاول is an adverbial phrase 'from the outset / at the beginning' (به = to/at, زاول = outset). سرآید is a third-person subjunctive/impersonal 'should come/befall'. زمان 'time/occasion' is the subject. Together: 'If an occasion were to come to me from the very outset...' (i.e. 'if at first such a time befell me').
segments.s409-g48: بدان سو is a compressed prepositional expression 'to that side/direction' (ب = به 'to', دان here part of the fused demonstrative 'بدان' = 'to that'). کشد is third-person singular present/future of کشیدن 'will draw/turn/move' (here 'will turn/move toward'). اخترم 'my star' (اختر + possessive -م). بی‌گمان 'undoubtedly' is an emphatic adverb. Altogether: 'my star will doubtless turn toward that side.'
segments.s409-g73: همه شب is an adverbial time phrase placed at the front meaning “the whole night / all night.” ز here is the causal/ablative preposition “out of / because of,” taking the abstract noun مهر “love/affection.” پسرِ مادرش is a genitive chain: پسر (son) followed by مادر with the possessive suffix ش (his/her), so “the son of his mother” (i.e. “the mother's son”).
segments.s409-g74: ز دیده is an ablative/locative phrase “from (the) eye(s).” همی before the verb is an archaic/poetic continuous marker (classical Persian) giving a habitual/continuing sense. ریخت is the verb root (from ریختن) used here with همی to mean “kept falling/flowing” (i.e. continuous past). خون بر برش is a prepositional phrase, with بر “upon/on” governing برش as its object (here meaning his face/cheek); the final word is not covered by the supplied senses list.
segments.s409-g9: ز introduces a source or origin ('from'); گیتی همی together mean 'in the world / always in the world' with همی functioning as an adverb 'always'. پند مادر is a noun phrase 'mother's advice' (پند = advice, مادر = mother). نیوش here is used adjectivally/qualitatively (literally 'tasting/enjoyment') to suggest 'pleasant/sweet' or attractively taken advice — a poetic use rather than the literal 'draught'.
segments.s409-g10: This clause gives the verbal advice. به introduces the direction/target ('toward'); بد تیز is a collocation 'evil (بد) swiftly/keenly (تیز)'. مشتاب is an imperative-form verb 'hurry!' (here understood negatively by the context of counsel: 'do not rush into...'), و links the following negative command, چندین 'many/several', and مکوش is a negative-impérative ('do not attempt / do not be').
segments.s409-g11: سواری 'rider/horseman' is modified by a relative clause introduced by که 'who/that'. باشد is the finite copula ('is/has') describing the rider; به + نیروی پیل is an instrumental expression 'by/with the strength of an elephant' (به here = 'by/with', نیروی = 'strength of', پیل = 'elephant').
segments.s409-g12: ز introduces the cause/instrument ('from' / 'by means of'); خون is the direct object (here physical blood or bloodshed). راند is a past tense verb 'drove/caused to flow'. اندر زمین 'into/in the earth/ground' sets the location, and جوی نیل is a simile 'a stream like the Nile' (جوی = stream, نیل = the Nile). The clause says blood was driven so that it ran over the ground like the Nile.
segments.s409-g13: بدرد here is used causatively/locative 'to cause pain / strike at', taking جگرگاه ('the region of the liver/heart', i.e. the innermost part) as the site. دیو سپید is the patient 'the white demon' (دیو = demon, سپید qualifying it as 'white/bright'). The whole phrase means he struck the white demon in the heart.
segments.s409-g14: ز شمشیر او uses ز instrumentally 'by/with his sword' (شمشیر = sword, او = his). گم کند is a causative construction 'makes [someone] lose / causes to be lost' (گم = lost, کند = makes/does). راه is the direct object 'the way/path', and شید (literally 'love‑mad' or 'the lovesick/beloved') names who loses the way; poetically, 'by his sword even the lover/lovesick goes astray.'
segments.s409-g15: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the very'; ماه here is used metaphorically for a beautiful person ('moon' = beautiful one). هاماوران is a poetic place‑name/descriptor 'the level plains' qualifying that moon. را is the definite object marker and بکشت is a past tense verb 'he killed/slew' (archaic form).
segments.s409-g16: نیارست here cannot be matched exactly to the provided noun senses; its function in context is verbal/valency: 'was not possible / could not'. گفتن 'to say' is the infinitive governing کس 'anyone/someone' as subject. او را is the object 'him', and درشت means 'rough/cruel'. The clause therefore means 'no one could say him to be cruel' (i.e. 'no one could call him cruel').
segments.s409-g49: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (classical/poetic use) introducing a subordinate time clause. رستم is the proper name (subject). بیاید is the 3rd-person singular verb 'comes' (classical present/past narrative form). به فرمان من literally 'at/to my command' uses به + noun to mark manner/agency, with من as possessor of فرمان.
segments.s409-g50: ز من نشنود سرد هرگز سخن is the main clause: ز + pronoun marks source ('from me'), نشنود is the negative 3rd-person present/past-form of شنیدن 'he does not hear / will not hear', سرد here means 'harsh/aloof' (a qualifying adjective for سخن), and هرگز is the adverb 'never' modifying the negated verb; سخن is the noun 'word/remark' (object). Word order follows classical Persian: source (ز من) before verb.
segments.s409-g51: ببارید is the perfective/past-form of باریدن 'to fall / to rain' used poetically to mean 'poured/fell'. خون is the patient noun 'blood'. از مژه مادرش is a prepositional phrase 'from (the) eyelash(es) of his mother' (مژه + possessive ش). The clause presents a vivid pictorial action: blood appears to fall from the mother's lashes.
segments.s409-g52: همه پاک functions adverbially here: همه 'entirely/all' and پاک 'completely/cleanly' intensify the action. بر کند is the compound verb (بر- as separable prefix + کند from کندن 'to pull/take off'); in epic style کند can mark a past or dramatic action 'she pulled off'. موی از سرش 'the hair from her head' is the object with possessive ش. Word order places manner/intensity (همه پاک) before the verb.
segments.s409-g53: بدو گفت = 'he said to him': بدو is the allative pronoun 'to him/unto it', and گفت is the narrative past 'he said' (used here to introduce direct speech). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone. ژنده is an insulting adjective 'ragged/mangy', پیل is 'elephant' (often figurative for a huge fellow), and ژیان 'of life' or 'living' qualifies پیل; together they form the vocative phrase.
segments.s409-g54: همی is the classical adverb marking continuous/habitual or emphatic aspect ('always/ever'). خوار is an adjective 'despised/humiliated'. گیری is a present-form (classical usage) of گرفتن 'to take/seize' and here gives a sense of 'you will suffer/receive' humiliation; in older epic language -ی ending can mark 2nd person present. ز + نیرو marks source/cause 'by/from (my) strength/force'. روان here means 'soul/spirit' or 'force' and is the affected domain. The clause is a threat: you will continually be humbled/overcome by force.
segments.s409-g55: نباشی is the negative of باشی (classical 2nd-person present/future sense) functioning as 'you will not be' / 'do not be' in this context. بسنده is an adjective/adverb 'sufficient/adequate' used predicatively; تو is the 2nd-person pronoun (emphatic here). با پیلتن 'with/against the Piltan (elephants)' uses با to indicate the opposing party; پیلتن is a place/name (here the elephants/forces of Piltan). The clause means 'You will not be sufficient against the Piltan elephants.'
segments.s409-g56: از ایدر مرو is an imperative-directional phrase: از ایدر 'from here', مرو is the classical imperative 'go' (here used in the negative sense 'do not go' — i.e., 'do not go from here to Merv' without something). بی یکی انجمن means 'without a single companion/company' (بی = without, یکی = one, انجمن = assembly/company). Together the clause commands: 'From here, do not go to Merv without at least one companion.'
segments.s409-g17: همانا is an emphatic particle meaning 'truly/verily'. چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the subordinate clause. سهراب دیگر سوار is a nominal clause 'Sohrab (no) longer a horseman' — note the copula is postponed to the next segment (the negative past نبوده/نبودست).
segments.s409-g18: نبودست is an older/past negative copula meaning 'there was not / was no longer' and completes the clause begun earlier. جنگی is used adjectivally/epithetically 'warlike' (describing the following word), گه means 'occasion/place' and کارزار is 'battle/field'; together the phrase says 'there was no warlike occasion/place of battle.'
segments.s409-g19: به + چنگ here marks motion/tendency into a hold: 'into the grasp'; چنگ 'grip, claw' is used metaphorically. پدر is the possessor 'father' and در is the locative 'in/within'; به هنگام is a fixed temporal phrase 'at the time of' followed by جنگ 'battle'. The whole segment sets time and circumstance: 'in his father's grasp at the time of battle.'
segments.s409-g20: به آوردگه is locative/inchoative 'at/to the place (of arrival/combat)'; کشته شد is the passive past 'was killed' (کشته + شد as auxiliary), and بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'immediately.' Word order places the location first, then the passive verb phrase, then the adverb of manner.
segments.s409-g21: به کین is a prepositional phrase 'out of/for vengeance' (به introducing the motive), followed by the object of that hatred سیاوش. ز before افراسیاب is the archaic poetic 'by/from' marking the agent responsible — here 'by Afrâsiyâb.'
segments.s409-g22: ز خون here means 'with/because of blood' (i.e., bloodshed). کرد is the simple past 'made/caused', گیتی is 'the world', چو is the simile 'like/as', دریای (from دریا) 'sea', and آب 'water' — together: 'he made the world like a sea of blood.'
segments.s409-g23: که here introduces a clause expressing a wish or conclusion. نفرین is 'a curse'; بر is the preposition 'upon'; این تخت و این تاج 'this throne and this crown' are its targets. باد at the end is the optative particle 'may it be' (a classical/poetic wish-marker).
segments.s409-g24: This parallel clause continues the optative with بر این کشتن 'upon this killing/slaughter' and شور و تاراج 'the tumult and pillage.' باد again is the optative 'may (it be).' The structure repeats the preposition بر and the wish-particle to place the curse on multiple evils.
segments.s409-g1: کتایون is the subject (proper name). چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning “when.” بشنید is the past tense ‘heard’ (1st/3rd-person past stem + ending omitted in poetry), and شد functions as the intransitive perfective ‘became.’ پر ز خشم is a predicative phrase ‘full of anger’ (پر as predicative adjective + ز ‘of/from’ before the emotive noun). Word order (Subject + چو + verb + شد + predicate) is typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s409-g2: به پیش پسر is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in the presence of / before the son’ (پیش = ‘in the presence of’; به marks direction/position). شد again is ‘became.’ پر از + noun is the usual construction ‘full of …’ where از links the adjective پر to its content. آب چشم is a figurative, poetic compound ‘water of the eye’ = tears. The clause therefore states a change of state in the son’s presence.
segments.s409-g3: چنین is an adverb meaning ‘thus/so.’ گفت here functions as the verb ‘said/spoke’ (poetic diction). با introduces the addressee (‘to/with’), so با فرخ اسفندیار = ‘to/with Farrokh Esfandiyar’ (فرخ is an epithet meaning ‘fortunate’ used with the name). Word order (adverb + verb + به/با + addressee) is common in narrative lines.
segments.s409-g4: که‌ای is a poetic combination here acting like a relative/vocative phrase ‘who art…’ or a complementizer introducing the appositive predication. از کیان جهان یادگار is a predicate: از + کیان = ‘of/from the Kayan (kings),’ جهان = ‘the world,’ and یادگار = ‘remnant/memorial’ (used predicatively). Together this yields ‘(you) who are a remnant of the Kayan kings of the world.’
segments.s409-g5: ز بهمن شنیدم is a reporting phrase: ز + proper name gives the source ‘from Bahman,’ and شنیدم is the first‑person past ‘I heard.’ که introduces the reported clause (‘that …’). از گلستان begins that clause with از = ‘from’ and گلستان = place name ‘Golestan.’
segments.s409-g6: همی is a classical progressive/emphatic particle (here marking ongoing or imminent action). رفت is the verbal stem ‘go/leave.’ خواهی acts as a modal/future auxiliary ‘you will/you intend’ — in prose it normally appears as خواهی رفت but is metrically inverted here (رفت خواهی). به زابلستان is the destination ‘to Zabulistan.’ Together they report the intention/future movement.
segments.s409-g7: ببندی is a second‑person form of بندن/بستن in classical/poetic Persian meaning ‘you will bind’ or an optative/imperative ‘bind!’ (the doubled b- is the classical present/future marker). همی adds continuous/emphatic nuance. رستم زال را is the definite direct object (‘Rostam, Zal’) marked by را. The verb precedes its object here due to poetic word order.
segments.s409-g8: خداوند is an honorific here meaning ‘lord/master’ (a title applied to the addressee). شمشیر و گوپال names what he is lord of (‘sword and Gopala’), with و linking them. The trailing را after گوپال is an emphatic/definite marker placed after the title phrase; in poetry such placement can mark vocative emphasis (‘O lord of the sword and Gopala’).
segments.s409-g57: مبر is a negative imperative meaning “do not” and here governs the following noun phrases; پیش is a preposition ‘before/in the presence of’ and combines with پیل ژیان (pیل = ‘elephant’, ژیان = ‘living/life’) as a prepositional phrase ‘before the living elephant’. هوش خویش is a possessive noun phrase ‘his/one’s own intellect’ (هوش = ‘intellect’, خویش = reflexive ‘one’s own’) and functions as the object of the implied action.
segments.s409-g58: نهاده is a perfective/participle meaning ‘placed/put’; بدین گونه is a fixed adverbial phrase ‘in this way’ (بدین = ‘by/with this’, گونه = ‘manner’). بر دوش خویش is a prepositional phrase ‘upon his/one’s own shoulder’ (بر = ‘on/upon’, دوش = ‘shoulder’, خویش + ش = reflexive possessive). The whole segment is a participial clause that links back to the subject/action introduced earlier.
segments.s409-g59: اگر introduces a conditional ‘if’. زین نشان is a prepositional/nominal phrase ‘by/with this sign’ (زین here functions as ‘this’/‘by this’ in poetic usage and نشان = ‘sign’). رای تو رفتنست is a verbless predicate where رفتنست is an archaic fused form meaning ‘is to go’ (رفتن + است), so the clause reads ‘your counsel/plan (رای تو) is to go’. The order reflects older/poetic Persian where the predicate follows the possessor phrase.
segments.s409-g60: همه کام is a nominative phrase ‘every/all desire’ (همه = ‘all’, کام = ‘desire’). بدگوهر is a descriptive adjective ‘ill‑natured/evil’ modifying کام, and آهرمنست is a nominal predicate meaning ‘is Ahriman / is of the evil spirit’ (آهرمن + است); the copula is contracted into the suffix ‑ست. The clause therefore asserts that all desire in that case is from Ahriman/the evil spirit.
segments.s409-g61: به دوزخ مبر is a negative imperative clause ‘do not take/lead into hell’ (به = ‘to/into’, دوزخ = ‘hell’, مبر = negative imperative ‘do not take’). کودکان را is the definite direct object marked by را (‘the children’). به پای is an adverbial/locative phrase literally ‘to the foot/base’ (پای used idiomatically), modifying the action of taking/leading.
segments.s409-g62: که here introduces a subordinate clause ‘that/for that (reason)’. دانا is the subject/agent ‘the wise (man)’. بخواند is a present/subjunctive-ish form ‘calls/calls out (names)’ (here used as ‘will call’ or ‘is apt to call’), with ترا را as its object ‘you’. پاک رای is the predicate nominal ‘pure in counsel’ (پاک = ‘pure’, رای = ‘counsel/opinion’), so the clause says the wise will call you ‘pure‑counsel’ (i.e. blame/praise in counsel).
segments.s409-g63: به مادر چنین گفت places the indirect object first in the dative construction ‘to the mother he said thus’ (به = ‘to’, مادر = ‘mother’, چنین گفت = ‘said thus’). پس جنگجوی follows as a topical/identifying tag ‘then the warrior’ (پس = ‘then/thereupon’, جنگجوی = ‘the warrior’), so the line names the speaker/context before giving the content clause.
segments.s409-g64: که introduces the reported content ‘that …’. نابردن is an infinitival/gerundial form ‘not to take’ (naf‘al-style negated infinitive), with کودکان as its logical object. نیست روی is a nominal copular clause where روی (‘face’) stands for ‘shame/disgrace/honor’ and نیست = ‘is not’, so نابردن کودکان نیست روی means ‘not taking children is not (a matter of) disgrace’ (i.e. it is not shameful to refrain from taking them).
segments.s409-g25: مده is a negative imperative ('do not give/put'); از پی is a compound prepositional idea 'in pursuit of / following' (از = 'from', پی = 'after, following'). تاج سر را is the definite object ('the crown of the head') with را marking it; به باد literal 'to the wind' forms the idiom 'to cast to the wind / to throw away' — together: 'Do not cast the crown of your head to the wind (i.e. risk your head for the crown).'
segments.s409-g26: که introduces the reason ('for/because'). با تاج شاهی = 'with the royal crown / the crown of kingship' (با = 'with', تاج شاهی = 'royal crown/kingship crown'). ز مادر نزاد is a poetic expression meaning 'was born of the mother' (نزاد = birth/being born); together: 'for (he) was born to the royal crown from his mother' — i.e. the kingship is by birth.
segments.s409-g27: پدر پیر is 'the (old) father' (adjective before noun). سر گشت uses گشت (past 'became') with سر ('head') to express 'his head became (grey)' — an idiom for 'he grew old'. و connects to the next clause; برنا توی places 'برنا' ('youthful') with توی ('in / among') — poetically implying 'you are young' or 'you are a youthful one among (people)'.
segments.s409-g28: به زور = 'in/with force' and then و (and) links another phrase; به مردی توانا توی = 'in manhood (مردی) powerful (توانا) among (توی)'. The preposition به marks the respect/measure ('in strength / by strength'), and توانا is an adjective applied to مردی, so the clause qualifies the youth as strong and capable.
segments.s409-g29: سپه (army) is subject, یکسره is an adverb 'completely/continually', بر تو is the prepositional object 'upon you'. چشم دارند is literally 'have eyes' — a standard Persian idiom meaning 'they keep watch' or 'they have their eyes on (you)'; دارَند is the present plural 'they have/hold'.
segments.s409-g30: میفگن is a present-tense verb 'they cast/throw' (an archaic/poetic present form). تن 'the body' is the direct object, اندر بلایی = 'into/within a calamity' (اندر = 'in/within', بلایی = 'calamity'), and به خشم 'in/with anger' gives the motive. Together: 'they cast the body into calamity in anger' i.e. they intend to bring bodily harm in wrath.
segments.s409-g31: جز از = 'except (from)'; سیستان names the place being excepted. در جهان جای هست is an existential phrase 'there is a place in the world' (در جهان = 'in the world', جای هست = 'a place exists'); in context the line restricts where bold action is appropriate — i.e. apart from Sistan there is no place (for such deeds). The word order is poetic but functions as an existential with an exception fronted.
segments.s409-g32: دلیری مکن = negative imperative 'do not (مکن) be brave/act bravely' (دلیری = bravery; can imply rashness). تیز منمای دست: تیز 'swift/sharp', منمای is negative imperative 'do not show/display' (منما(ی) = 'do not show'), and دست 'hand' stands for action/force — 'do not show a swift hand' meaning 'do not act rashly or strike quickly.'
segments.s409-g65: چو introduces a subordinate temporal clause meaning 'when/if'. The phrase orders the elements poetically: با زن 'with a woman', پس پرده 'behind the curtain/screen' (پس here meaning 'after/behind' before پرده), and باشد is the copula/verb ('is' in a narrative/subjunctive register) linking to the predicate جوان, which here functions as the subject/predicate 'young man'.
segments.s409-g66: بماند is a present/subjunctive/resultative verb meaning 'remains' or 'is left' and states a continuing condition; منش پست و تیره‌روان is the predicate describing that continuing state ('a base/groveling and ill‑fated disposition'). Adjectives follow the noun in Persian, and the verb is placed before the descriptive phrase here for poetic inversion.
segments.s409-g67: به هر رزمگه is a locative/prepositional phrase 'at/in every battlefield'. باید is a modal auxiliary 'must/ought to'. او را is the third‑person object/dative with the object marker را. نگاه here is a noun 'watch/gaze' that combines with the following verb to form the idiom 'to set/keep a watch on someone' (نگاه + گذارد). Word order places the modal (باید) before the object and the light‑noun + verb compound.
segments.s409-g68: گذارد is the main verb 'place/put' (here completing نگاه گذارد 'to set/keep watch'). بهر is a preposition 'for/on behalf of' introducing purpose/benefit, followed by زخم 'wound' and the name گوپال شاه 'King Gopal' (name + title placed after the noun). The phrase means the watch is for the sake of King Gopal's wound (i.e., to tend/attend to that wound).
segments.s409-g69: مرا (here split in the tokens as م + را) functions as the dative/accusative 'to/for me' (را is the object marker). لشکری خود 'my/one's own army' (خود marks possession/emphasis). نیاید به کار is an idiom meaning 'does not come to use / is of no use' — نیاید is negative 'does not come' and به کار 'to (the) use', so together 'is of no use to me'. The verb phrase is third‑person negative and the overall word order places the experiencer (مرا) before the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s409-g70: جز از marks the exception 'except from/for', followed by a list: خوی + ش is 'his/one's own kin' (خوی = kin/kinship, ش as the possessive suffix), پیوند here means 'allies/connected men' (literally 'bond/link' used as allies), and چندی سوار 'a few horsemen' (چندی 'some/several' modifying سوار 'riders/horsemen'). The coordinating و links the items in the exception list.
segments.s409-g71: ز پیش is a poetic locative 'from/in front of' (ز = from, پیش = before/in front of). پسر مادر مهربان is a genitive/noun phrase 'the mother's kind son' (پسر 'son' + مادر 'mother' as possessor, with مهربان 'kind' qualifying the mother); Persian places the possessor and its modifier after the possessed noun in this poetic ordering.
segments.s409-g72: بیامد is simple past 'came' (3rd person singular). پر از is the construction 'full of' (پر 'full' + از 'of'), taking درد 'pain/sorrow' as its object; و تیره‌روان adds a coordinating adjective 'and sorrowful/ill‑fated' describing his emotional state. Together they form the predicate 'came, full of pain and sorrowful.'
segments.s409-g33: مرا (م + را) is the patient/object ‘me’ marked by the object marker را. خاکسار is a predicative noun meaning ‘humble supplicant’ placed after the object for emphasis. دو گیتی is a genitive phrase ‘of the two worlds’ modifying خاکسار. مکن is the negative imperative (مَکن = “do not make/do”), so the whole clause is a plea: “Do not make me the humbled one of both worlds.”
segments.s409-g34: ازین (از + این) = ‘from/of this’ (از = from; ین here is orthographic variant of این). مهربان modifies مام = ‘kind mother’ (dialectal word for mother). بشنو is the imperative ‘listen/heed’ and سخن is its object ‘word(s)/speech’; the verb imperative precedes the object here for force: “Hear/heed the speech from this kind mother.”
segments.s409-g35: چنین is an adverb ‘thus/so’ modifying پاسخ ‘answer’. آوردش = آورد + ش (enclitic) is a past-tense verbal form meaning ‘(he) brought/uttered it’ or ‘gave such a reply’; the enclitic ش refers to the answer (or ‘to him/it’) — the whole phrase = “Esfandiyar brought (gave) such an answer.”
segments.s409-g36: که‌ای here functions as an archaic/vocative address (که ای ≈ “O” / “O you”), followed by مهربان ‘kind/merciful’ as the addressee. این سخن = ‘this speech/these words’. یاددار is an archaic verbal form meaning ‘remember / keep in mind’ (imperative), so the quoted address reads “O kind one, remember these words.”
segments.s409-g37: همانست is the contracted form of همان است ‘that is / it is the same’. رستم is the identifier. که introduces the clause ‘which/that’ and دانی is the second-person present ‘you know’ (classical ending), with همی as an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning ‘indeed / as you always know’. Together: “That is Rostam, as you know.”
segments.s409-g38: هنرهاش = هنرها + اش ‘his arts/skills’ (possessive clitic). چون here functions as a particle meaning roughly ‘how’ or ‘the way that’. زند is an archaic/poetic verbal form (not one of the provided noun senses) expressing the action of striking/doing/putting into effect—here used to indicate how the skills are manifested (sense not listed). خوانی is the verb ‘(he) sings/recites/performs’ (here: performs/puts on display). همی is the usual classical particle adding continuity/emphasis (‘does so’ / ‘always’). The clause describes how his skills are shown when he acts/ performs.
segments.s409-g39: نکوکارتر is the comparative form ‘more virtuous’ (نکوکار + تر). ز (here in combination with following و/pronoun) functions as the comparative marker ‘than’ (poetic form of از). The sequence به ایران کسی literally orders the locative and the indefinite: ‘anyone in Iran’ (کسی as ‘anyone’). The overall sense: ‘No one in Iran is more virtuous than him.’
segments.s409-g40: نیابی is the negative present/subjunctive form from یافتن ‘to find’ (ن + یابی) and here works as ‘you will not find’ / ‘you do not find’. و‌گر is a conditional particle ‘if / even if’. چند is an indefinite/adverb of quantity ‘how much / however many’. پویی in this line is a poetic/formal form related to seeking/traveling (here ‘you seek/search’), but the provided senses did not include that verbal meaning. بسی is an adverb meaning ‘much / plenty / far’ and strengthens the idea of searching widely: ‘even if you search much/far and wide’.

sentences.s409-s21: If it is not proper to bind him, it would be unseemly — not fitting — for such a thing to come from the king.
sentences.s409-s22: But my heart must not be broken; for if you break my heart, I will separate myself from life.
sentences.s409-s23: How could I lift my head against the king's command? How could I abandon such an establishment (order)?
sentences.s409-s24: If, from the very outset, such a time should befall me, my star would surely turn toward that side.
sentences.s409-s37: All night, out of love for the mother's son, blood kept flowing from his eyes onto his face.
sentences.s409-s5: Always heed a mother's counsel: do not hasten swiftly toward evil, and do not attempt many things.
sentences.s409-s6: A rider who has the strength of an elephant drives blood across the ground like a Nile-stream.
sentences.s409-s7: He wounded the white demon at the heart; by his sword he makes the lovesick lose their way.
sentences.s409-s8: He slew that moon of the level plains; no one could call him cruel.
sentences.s409-s25: When Rostam comes at my command, he will never hear a harsh word from me.
sentences.s409-s26: Blood poured from his mother's eyelashes; she tore all the hair cleanly from her head.
sentences.s409-s27: He said to him, 'O mangy elephant of life!' — 'You will continually be humiliated; you will be overpowered by (my) strength/soul.'
sentences.s409-s28: You will not suffice against the Piltan elephants; from here do not go to Merv without at least one companion.
sentences.s409-s9: Truly, when Sohrab was no longer a horseman, there remained no place for battle.
sentences.s409-s10: Caught in his father's grasp at the time of battle, he was slain on the field at once.
sentences.s409-s11: Out of vengeance for Siyâvash, Afrâsiyâb made the world like a sea of blood.
sentences.s409-s12: May a curse be upon this throne and this crown; upon this slaughter and upon the tumult and pillage may there be doom.
sentences.s409-s1: When Katayun heard, she was filled with anger; in her son's presence she was filled with tears.
sentences.s409-s2: Thus he spoke to Farrokh Esfandiyar: 'You are a remnant of the Kayan kings of the world.'
sentences.s409-s3: I heard from Bahman that you intend to leave Golestan and go to Zabulistan.
sentences.s409-s4: You will bind Rostam and Zal, O lord of the sword and of Gopala.
sentences.s409-s29: Do not set your wit before the living elephant; having placed it in this way upon your own shoulder.
sentences.s409-s30: If by this sign your counsel is to go, then every desire is from the evil spirit (Ahriman).
sentences.s409-s31: Do not lead the children to hell at the feet/base, for the wise will call you ‘pure in counsel’.
sentences.s409-s32: He thus said to the mother — then the warrior — that not taking the children is no disgrace.
sentences.s409-s13: Do not cast your head to the wind in pursuit of the crown, for the crown of kingship is not born of the mother.
sentences.s409-s14: Your father has grown old, and you are a youth — in strength and in manhood you are powerful.
sentences.s409-s15: The whole army keeps its eyes fixed on you; in anger they intend to hurl the body into calamity.
sentences.s409-s16: There is no place in the world except Sistan; do not be brave, do not display a swift hand.
sentences.s409-s33: When a young man is behind the curtain with a woman, his character remains base and ill‑fated.
sentences.s409-s34: At every battlefield one must set watch over him for the wound of King Gopal.
sentences.s409-s35: My own army will be of no use to me except my kin, allies, and a few horsemen.
sentences.s409-s36: Before her, the mother's kind son came, full of pain and sorrowful.
sentences.s409-s17: Do not make me the humbled one of the two worlds; listen to the words of this kind mother.
sentences.s409-s18: Esfandiyar answered thus: “O kind one, remember these words.”
sentences.s409-s19: That is Rostam, as you know; his skills are such when he displays them.
sentences.s409-s20: You will find no one in Iran more virtuous than him, even if you search far and wide.
